skill.name: render-automation
skill.description: Automate Render service discovery, deployments, deployment status checks, and project listing through the Rube MCP Render toolkit.
skill.summary: Use Rube MCP to find Render services, trigger deploys, monitor deploy status, and list projects.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The shell execution and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code spans and ordinary Render field names. The real risk is operational: the skill directs users to connect an external Rube MCP endpoint and can trigger Render deployments after user authentication.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Endpoint Controls Render Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add the Rube MCP endpoint and authenticate a Render connection. This is a legitimate integration pattern, but it gives the connected tool access to list services, list projects, trigger deployments, and retrieve deployment status, so users should review permissions before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly documents connection to an external MCP endpoint and Render deployment workflows. The behavior is intended and user-authorized, so this is an operational risk rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Shell Execution Static Findings Are Markdown References
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backticked strings as Ruby or shell backtick execution. In context, these are Markdown references to MCP tool names, parameter names, enum values, and workflow examples, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is Markdown instructions and the flagged text is inside inline code formatting or fenced workflow examples. No Ruby file, shell script, or executable command invocation is present in the cited locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography And Reconnaissance Alerts Are Unsubstantiated
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts do not match the semantic content. The cited lines describe the Render service, deployment response fields, ID extraction, polling intervals, and API rate limits.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text contains no cryptographic algorithm use and no host, process, or network reconnaissance instructions. The alerts appear to come from keyword matching rather than executable behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Authorized Deployment Triggering
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can guide an agent to trigger Render deployments after resolving a service ID. This is expected for deployment automation but can affect production services if used without change controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deployment workflow is explicitly documented and requires a Render service ID. The risk depends on the permissions granted through the connected Render account.

content.value_statement: Render operations often require repeated service lookup, deploy triggering, and status checks. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for those tasks through Rube MCP.
actual_capabilities: Connects a user to the Rube MCP Render toolkit workflow.
Lists Render services with name, type, limit, and cursor filters.
Triggers manual deployments for resolved Render service IDs.
Retrieves deployment status using service and deployment IDs.
Lists Render projects with cursor-based pagination.
Documents exact service type values and deployment status meanings.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP to be configured in the client.
Requires an active user-authorized Render connection.
Does not create, edit, or delete Render services.
Depends on current Rube tool schemas returned at runtime.

use_cases.title: Deploy a Web Service
use_cases.description: Find a Render service by name, trigger a deployment, and monitor the result until it reaches a terminal state.

use_cases.title: Review Deployment Health
use_cases.description: Check a deployment ID for live, failed, canceled, or in-progress status without navigating the Render dashboard.

use_cases.title: Inventory Render Resources
use_cases.description: List services and projects with pagination to understand what exists in a Render account.

prompt_templates.title: List My Render Services
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Render automation skill to list my Render services. Search the Rube tools first, then show service names, IDs, and service types.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy One Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the Render service named [service name], trigger a deployment without clearing the cache, and return the deploy ID and initial status.

prompt_templates.title: Monitor a Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Check deployment [deploy ID] for service [service ID]. Report the current status, timestamps, and whether action is needed.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Projects and Services
prompt_templates.prompt: List all Render projects and services using pagination. Group services by project when possible and identify services without a project.

output_examples.output: A concise table of matching services with names, service IDs, service types, and pagination notes.

output_examples.input: Deploy the production API service and monitor it.
output_examples.output: A deployment summary with resolved service ID, deploy ID, current status, and next polling recommendation.
A project inventory with project names, project IDs, and whether more pages are available.

best_practices: Search Rube tools first so current schemas and parameters are used.
Resolve service names to service IDs before triggering deployments.
Poll deployments every 10 to 30 seconds to respect Render rate limits.
anti_patterns: Do not trigger production deployments without confirming the target service.
Do not assume service names are unique when multiple matches are returned.
Do not poll deployment status in rapid loops.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Render API key?
faq.answer: No direct API key is documented. It uses a user-authorized Render connection through Rube MCP.

faq.question: Can it create new Render services?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows cover listing services, triggering deployments, checking deployments, and listing projects.

faq.question: Why must it search tools first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change. Searching first helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use current parameters.

faq.question: Can it clear the build cache?
faq.answer: Yes. The deployment workflow documents a clearCache option for clean builds when needed.

faq.question: What deployment statuses does it understand?
faq.answer: It documents created, build in progress, update in progress, live, deactivated, failed, and canceled states.

faq.question: Is it safe for production accounts?
faq.answer: Use caution. It can trigger deployments through authorized Render access, so confirm targets and permissions before running workflows.
